attributes.stem: A 46-year-old woman presents with a two month history of a painless abdominal swelling. The mass is most noticeable when she stands up or when she coughs. The patient appears to be generally well, without any obvious signs of pain. Her only other health issues are a deep venous thrombosis in her left leg, which occurred after a laparoscopic appendicectomy three months ago. She is taking warfarin for this. On examination, her vital signs are within normal range. Her abdomen is soft, with no areas of localised tenderness. The mass is only evident when she strains. Auscultation reveals normal bowel sounds.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with an abdominal mass that is most prominent when standing or coughing, which suggests that it could be a hernia. Based on its location being on the lateral border of the rectus sheath and below the umbilicus, a Spigelian hernia is the most likely diagnosis. These hernia occur lateral to the rectus sheath, in a defect to the transversus aponeurosis. A surgical intervention is required as this type of hernia is at risk of incarceration and strangulation.

Whilst incisional hernias are relatively common causes of anterior abdominal wall swellings, such a defect through a small laparoscopic port site would be very uncommon.

A rectus sheath haematoma should always be considered as a diagnostic possibility for an anterior abdominal wall swelling - particularly in patients on warfarin. This scenario tends to occur when the patient starts taking another medication that interferes with warfarin metabolism and the INR increases and spontaneous haemorrhage becomes a real risk. However, these haematomas in the confined space of the rectus sheath are usually painful and tender on palpation.

A lipoma fits with the description of a painless, slow-growing abdominal mass and would probably be the commonest cause of such a swelling. Nonetheless, as this patient's mass is more pronounced when the patient stands up, it is most likely to be a hernia.

A panniculus is defined as excess skin and fat hanging from the abdomen in a apron-like fashion. This finding is common in overweight individuals and would be bilateral.
learningPoints: Spigelian hernias are located on the lower lateral border of the rectus abdomins.
Urgent surgical intervention is required to prevent incarceration and strangulation of Spigelian hernias.
references: Webber V, Low C, Skipworth RJE, Kumar S, et al. Contemporary thoughts on the management of Spigelian hernia. Hernia. 2017;21(3):355–61. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28097450/

Hanzalova I, Schäfer M, Demartines N, Clerc D. Spigelian hernia: current approaches to surgical treatment—a review. Hernia [Internet]. 2021 Oct 19 [cited 2022 Nov 23]; Available at: https://link.springer.com/10.1007/s10029-021-02511-8

Sachs D, Sequeira Campos M, Murray J. Panniculectomy. [Updated 2022 Oct 2]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499822/

Bernd Kasper, Philipp Ströbel, Peter Hohenberger, Desmoid Tumors: Clinical Features and Treatment Options for Advanced Disease. The Oncologist,.  2011.16(5), Issue 5, May 2011, Pages: 682–693. Available at: https://academic.oup.com/oncolo/article/16/5/682/6400867
